verses.commentary: Very early morning. Chief priests, elders, teachers, whole Sanhedrin made plans. They bound Jesus, led him away, handed him to Pilate. Formal council decision. Roman governor required for execution. Jews cannot execute under Roman law. They deliver Jesus to the Gentiles, as he predicted (10:33).
Pilate: Are you the king of the Jews? Political question. Jesus: You have said so. Affirmation with qualification. Yes, but not as Pilate thinks.
Chief priests accused him of many things. Accusations multiply. Details unstated. Slander accumulates.
Pilate again: Aren't you going to answer? See how many things they accuse you of. Pilate expects defense. Silence baffles him.
But Jesus made no reply. Pilate was amazed. Sovereign silence continues. Isaiah 53:7. The Lamb does not protest.
Custom at the festival: release a prisoner whom the people requested. Passover amnesty. Political gesture.
Barabbas was in prison with insurrectionists who committed murder in an uprising. Real criminal. Violent rebel. Guilty of what they accuse Jesus.
The crowd came, asked Pilate to do what he usually did. They want the amnesty.
Pilate: Do you want me to release the king of the Jews? He offers Jesus. Sees a way out.
Knowing it was out of self-interest that chief priests handed Jesus over. Pilate perceives envy, not justice. Political calculation, not moral conviction.
Chief priests stirred up the crowd to have Pilate release Barabbas instead. Manipulation. The leaders turn the people. Mob incited.
What shall I do with the one you call king of the Jews? Pilate still seeks escape. He wants them to decide.
Crucify him! The shout. Unanimous demand. Death penalty.
Why? What crime has he committed? Pilate asks. But they shouted louder: Crucify him! No answer to the question. Volume replaces reason.

Wanting to satisfy the crowd, Pilate released Barabbas. He had Jesus flogged and handed him over to be crucified. Barabbas goes free; Jesus takes his place. Substitution enacted. The guilty released, the innocent condemned. Roman flogging was brutal: leather whips with bone and metal, tearing flesh. Pilate, the judge, becomes the executioner.
Soldiers led Jesus into the palace (Praetorium), called together the whole company. Mob of soldiers. Collective cruelty.
They put a purple robe on him, twisted together a crown of thorns, set it on him. Mock coronation. Purple = royalty. Thorns = curse (Genesis 3:18). Pain as parody.
They called out: Hail, king of the Jews! Sarcastic homage. Truth spoken in mockery.
Again and again they struck him on the head with a staff, spit on him. Falling on their knees, they paid homage. Worship as violence. Feigned reverence, real brutality.
When they had mocked him, they took off the purple robe, put his own clothes on him. Then they led him out to crucify him. Play ends. Execution begins.

A man from Cyrene, Simon, father of Alexander and Rufus, passing by from the country, was forced to carry the cross. Simon named, sons named. Known to Mark's audience (Romans 16:13?). Jesus too weak to carry the crossbeam. Simon conscripted. He bears Jesus' cross literally; disciples will bear it figuratively (8:34).
They brought Jesus to Golgotha (place of the skull). Outside the city walls. Place of execution. Death and desolation.
They offered him wine mixed with myrrh, but he did not take it. Analgesic to dull pain. He refuses. He will drink the cup fully, consciously.
And they crucified him. Three words. Brutal simplicity. Mark does not dwell on details. Dividing his clothes, they cast lots. Psalm 22:18 fulfilled. Soldiers gamble while he dies.
Timing noted. Passover lambs will be slaughtered at 3 PM. Jesus precedes them.
The written notice of the charge: THE KING OF THE JEWS. Pilate's verdict becomes proclamation. Irony: the charge is the truth. He dies for claiming what he is.
They crucified two rebels with him, one on his right, one on his left. Isaiah 53:12: numbered with the transgressors. The sinless one dies between sinners.
Verse missing in most manuscripts. Later addition from Luke 22:37: And the scripture was fulfilled that says, He was numbered with the transgressors.
Those passing by hurled insults, shaking their heads: So! You who are going to destroy the temple and build it in three days...
If/then theology. Prove power by escape. They misunderstand: staying on the cross is the proof.

Chief priests and teachers mocked among themselves: He saved others, but can't save himself! Irony. True statement, wrong conclusion. He can save himself but won't. Saving others requires not saving himself.

Let this Messiah, king of Israel, come down now from the cross, that we may see and believe. Demand for sign. But they will not believe. Those crucified with him also insulted him. Universal rejection. Even the dying criminals mock.
Sixth to ninth hour. Three hours of black. Amos 8:9: I will make the sun go down at noon. Creation mourns. Or God hides his face.

At three in the afternoon Jesus cried out in a loud voice: Eloi, Eloi, lema sabachthani? My God, my God, why have you forsaken me? Psalm 22:1. Aramaic preserved. The cry of dereliction. The Son experiences abandonment. God-forsakenness. The Father turns away as the Son bears sin. Deepest mystery of the atonement. Infinite cost.
Some standing near: Listen, he's calling Elijah. Misunderstanding. Eloi sounds like Elijah. Even his final cry is misheard.
Someone ran, filled a sponge with wine vinegar, put it on a staff, offered it to Jesus. Now leave him alone. Let's see if Elijah comes to take him down. Psalm 69:21. Mockery continues to the end.
Death. The Son of God dies. The King expires. Mission accomplished.
From top to bottom: God's act, not human. Access to God's presence now open. The way is cleared. Hebrews 10:19–20. The old order ends.

The centurion, who stood in front of Jesus, saw how he died, said: Surely this man was the Son of God! A Gentile confesses what Israel denied. The executioner recognizes the victim's identity. First full confession in Mark. Irony: at the moment Jesus appears most God-forsaken, a pagan declares him God's Son.
Some women watching from a distance. Mary Magdalene, Mary mother of James and Joseph, Salome. Witnesses. The men fled; the women remain.
In Galilee these women followed him and cared for his needs. Many other women who came up with him to Jerusalem were there. Faithful disciples. They supported his ministry, now witness his death.
It was Preparation Day (day before the Sabbath). Evening approached. Urgency. Bodies must be buried before Sabbath begins at sundown.

Joseph of Arimathea, prominent Council member, himself waiting for the kingdom of God, went boldly to Pilate, asked for Jesus' body. A Sanhedrin member, but a secret disciple. Boldness in the crisis. He risks reputation, asks for a criminal's corpse.
Pilate surprised Jesus was already dead. He summoned the centurion, asked if Jesus had already died. Crucifixion usually took days. Jesus' death was rapid. Verification required.
When he learned from the centurion it was so, he gave the body to Joseph. Permission granted. The body released.

Joseph bought linen cloth, took down the body, wrapped it in linen, placed it in a tomb cut out of rock. Then he rolled a stone against the entrance. Proper burial. Isaiah 53:9: assigned a grave with the rich. The tomb sealed.

They know the location. They will return. Chapter fifteen: trial, mockery, crucifixion, death, burial. Pilate weak, Barabbas freed, Jesus crucified. Darkness descends, the curtain tears, a centurion confesses, women witness. The King dies. The world's sin judged. The way to God opened. It is finished.
